(**μSA**), as defined by the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") (OMB), are labor market and statistical areas in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") centered on an urban cluster (urban area) with a population of at least 10,000 but fewer than 50,000 people.[[1]](#cite_note-census.gov-1) The micropolitan area designation was created in 2003. Like the better-known [metropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area"), a micropolitan area is a [geographic](/wiki/Geographic "Geographic") entity used for [statistical](/wiki/Statistical "Statistical") purposes based on [counties and county equivalents](/wiki/List_of_United_States_counties_and_county_equivalents "List of United States counties and county equivalents").[[1]](#cite_note-census.gov-1) On July 21, 2023, the Office of Management and Budget released revised delineations of the various [core-based statistical areas](/wiki/Core-based_statistical_area "Core-based statistical area") (CBSAs) in the United States, which recognized 542 micropolitan areas in the United States, four of which are in [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico").[[2]](#cite_note-revision2023-2)

**United States micropolitan statistical areas** (**μSA**), as defined by the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") (OMB), are labor market and statistical areas in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") centered on an urban cluster (urban area) with a population of at least 10,000 but fewer than 50,000 people.[[1]](#cite_note-census.gov-1) The micropolitan area designation was created in 2003. Like the better-known [metropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area"), a micropolitan area is a [geographic](/wiki/Geographic "Geographic") entity used for [statistical](/wiki/Statistical "Statistical") purposes based on [counties and county equivalents](/wiki/List_of_United_States_counties_and_county_equivalents "List of United States counties and county equivalents").[[1]](#cite_note-census.gov-1) On July 21, 2023, the Office of Management and Budget released revised delineations of the various [core-based statistical areas](/wiki/Core-based_statistical_area "Core-based statistical area") (CBSAs) in the United States, which recognized 542 micropolitan areas in the United States, four of which are in [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico").[[2]](#cite_note-revision2023-2)

The term "micropolitan" gained currency in the 1990s to describe growing population centers in the United States that are removed from larger cities, in some cases by 100 miles (160 km) or more.

Micropolitan cities do not have the economic or political importance of large cities, but are nevertheless significant centers of population and production, drawing workers and shoppers from a wide local area. Because the designation is based on the core urban cluster's population and not on that of the whole area, some micropolitan areas are much larger than some metropolitan areas. For example, the [Ottawa, IL Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Ottawa,_IL_Micropolitan_Statistical_Area "Ottawa, IL Micropolitan Statistical Area") had a 2010 census population of 154,908. That would put its total population ahead of roughly 100 individual locations classified as a metropolitan statistical area in 2010. The largest of the areas, around [Claremont](/wiki/Claremont,_New_Hampshire "Claremont, New Hampshire") and [Lebanon, New Hampshire](/wiki/Lebanon,_New_Hampshire "Lebanon, New Hampshire"), had a population in excess of 218,000 in 2010; Claremont's population was only 13,355 in the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"),[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2010-3) and Lebanon's population was only 13,151.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
